critique.summary: The article proposes classifying happiness as a psychiatric disorder, termed 'major affective disorder, pleasant type.' It argues that happiness is statistically abnormal, involves a distinct cluster of symptoms, is linked to cognitive abnormalities, and may reflect central nervous system dysfunction, dismissing objections based on the positive valuation of happiness.
mainPoints: Happiness is statistically abnormal compared to typical mental states.
Happiness consists of a discrete cluster of symptoms similar to psychiatric conditions.
Happiness is associated with cognitive abnormalities.
Happiness likely reflects abnormal central nervous system functioning.
The objection that happiness is not negatively valued is considered scientifically irrelevant.
critique.truthfulness: The article presents a provocative and unconventional hypothesis rather than an established scientific consensus. While it cites relevant literature on affect and psychiatric classification, the proposal to classify happiness as a disorder is not supported by mainstream psychiatry or diagnostic manuals such as the DSM or ICD. Happiness is generally understood as a positive emotional state rather than a pathological condition. The claim that happiness is statistically abnormal and linked to cognitive abnormalities is not widely accepted or substantiated by robust empirical evidence. The dismissal of the positive value of happiness as irrelevant is a philosophical stance rather than a scientific conclusion.
considerations: Happiness is typically considered a positive and desirable emotional state in psychology and psychiatry.
Current diagnostic manuals do not recognize happiness as a disorder; mental health classifications focus on distress and impairment.
The article does not address the social and cultural context of happiness and mental health.
The proposal challenges fundamental assumptions about mental health and well-being without broad empirical support.
Ethical implications of pathologizing happiness are not discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around a 1992 proposal to classify happiness as a psychiatric disorder, with most commenters viewing it as a satirical critique of psychiatric over-diagnosis and cultural misconceptions about happiness. Several highlight the unrealistic expectation of constant happiness and the importance of distinguishing it from other emotional states.

comments.author: jayd16
comments.text: I think the DSM 5 says a disorder must cause clinically significant distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately references DSM-5 criteria, providing a factual basis to counter the proposal of classifying happiness as a disorder.

comments.author: tss93
comments.text:
The critique feels valid to me. There’s a tendency in modern psychology/media to pathologize the average human baseline: if you’re not consistently optimistic and thriving, something must be wrong with you, or at least you need to be in a pursuit of this.

But constant happiness isn’t realistic, it’s like a desire to be permanently high. From my own experience I’ve landed somewhere near the Buddhist framing: the healthy default is just calm and neutral, with happiness and sadness coming and going away.

Trying to force happiness as a permanent state seems like its own problem, which is kind of what Bentall is pointing at from the other direction.
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced and thoughtful critique of cultural and psychological expectations around happiness, aligning well with psychological understanding.

comments.author: bensyverson
comments.text:
Ha, this is fun. But there's a kernel of truth to it. The problem with American culture specifically is that it treats "happiness" as a goal, rather than a fleeting feeling that is probably better described with a more specific word (joy, accomplishment, excitement, satisfaction, contentment). Our culture leans on this so hard that people start to think there's something wrong with them if they're not feeling generalized happiness most of the time.

That's just not how life works.
comments.analysis: Insightfully critiques cultural attitudes toward happiness, emphasizing the complexity of emotional states beyond a simplistic goal.

comments.author: eouw0o83hf
comments.text:
I really liked this paper. I think it's less of an outright joke that it's possible to squint your eyes and laugh that happiness could be a disorder, and more of shining a light on the psychopathological system that tends towards over-diagnosis and hyperfixation on those diagnoses.

"If our so-called scientific system were really objective and honest, it would include happiness as a disorder." I think this is the goal the paper is trying to expose, more than just making a joke about mapping a good feeling to a description of a bad feeling. Indeed, I think the last line of the paper gives it away - our current system is very incomplete and needs to be extended:

> Indeed, only a psychopathology that openly declares the relevance of values to classification could persist in excluding happiness from the psychiatric disorders.
comments.analysis: Offers a meta-analysis of the paper's intent, highlighting its critique of psychiatric classification systems and their limitations.

comments.author: arizen
comments.text:
Happiness is a derivative of purpose. If someone optimizes their life strictly for happiness while deprioritizing purpose, they likely won't achieve either.

Pursuing a meaningful goal almost always requires enduring unpleasant phases and friction along the way.
comments.analysis: Provides a philosophical perspective linking happiness to meaningful goals, which is consistent with psychological theories on well-being.

comments.author: AnimalMuppet
comments.text:
<checks calendar> Wait, this isn't April 1st!

Seriously, happiness is a psychiatric disorder? Rare, sure, but a disorder? That's the craziest thing I've heard since... well, since the Iran war, I guess, so not very long. Still, that's nuts. I cannot imagine the world view that it must take to look at happiness that way.
comments.analysis: Expresses disbelief and emotional reaction to the proposal, reflecting common skepticism about pathologizing positive emotions.

comments.author: 8bitsrule
comments.text:
The way to happiness is to stop chasing it.

Never mind all the ads ... It isn't 'out there somewhere'.
comments.analysis: Offers a concise philosophical insight into happiness, aligning with common psychological advice on acceptance and non-striving.

comments.author: letharion
comments.text: I'm assuming this is some kind of jab at the general propensity of psychiatry to classify most things as disorders, rather than a serious proposal. If anything, I think the problem has gotten worse since this was published. (Then again, maybe happiness has also gotten more rare since 1992?)
comments.analysis: Correctly interprets the proposal as satirical and critiques the trend of over-diagnosis in psychiatry.

comments.author: iberator
comments.text:
Overall happinesses and motivation and belief are signs of too high level dopamine.

Most business owner people have it. That's why they are often out of touch with random Joe.

They belive in success even if math is saying that's bias.

Form of pychosis
comments.analysis: Makes speculative and unsupported claims about dopamine and psychosis in business owners, lacking scientific backing.

comments.author: _doctor_love
comments.text:
> It is proposed that happiness be classified as a psychiatric disorder and be included in future editions of the major diagnostic manuals under the new name: major affective disorder, pleasant type. In a review of the relevant literature it is shown that happiness is statistically abnormal, consists of a discrete cluster of symptoms, is associated with a range of cognitive abnormalities, and probably reflects the abnormal functioning of the central nervous system. One possible objection to this proposal remains--that happiness is not negatively valued. However, this objection is dismissed as scientifically irrelevant.

Reading this I can't help but feel that the person who wrote it is a POS.
comments.analysis: Expresses personal insult without constructive critique, reducing the value of the comment.

comments.author: pogue
comments.text:
This reminds me of this old gem from The Onion:

FDA Approves Depressant Drug For The Annoyingly Cheerful [video/NSFW/2:06] https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jd4tugPM83c
comments.analysis: Provides a humorous but irrelevant reference that does not contribute to the discussion's accuracy or depth.

comments.author: delichon
comments.analysis: Makes a sarcastic and off-topic joke that does not add meaningful insight to the discussion.

comments.author: dmschulman
comments.text: Woosh
comments.analysis: A brief dismissive comment likely indicating a missed joke, adding no substantive content.

comments.author: adyashakti
comments.text: it's Catch-22. the world is such a mess that if you're happy, you must be delusional.
comments.analysis: Offers a cynical but insightful comment on societal attitudes toward happiness, though somewhat emotional.

comments.author: emsign
comments.text: If you are too happy to work, you are sick. Makes sense.
comments.analysis: Sarcastic remark that critiques the absurdity of pathologizing happiness but lacks constructive depth.

comments.author: gabrielso
comments.text: Good news is that the government can offer free treatment.
comments.analysis: A sarcastic comment that does not contribute meaningful analysis or accuracy.

comments.author: kusokurae
comments.text:
Reminded of that episode of House where the lady with dormant syphillis had something like this.

I wonder are there any ways I can contract this without breaking marital vows
comments.analysis: Off-topic and inappropriate joke that detracts from the discussion's seriousness.

comments.author: rglover
comments.text: "It's so, so sad, to be happy all the time." - https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rzZPxUiAKTo
comments.analysis: A quote and link that add little analytical value to the discussion.

comments.author: techblueberry
comments.text: Ahh 1992.  At the time he probably didn’t know he needed to add a /s or he’d be taken seriously in our delusional future.
comments.analysis: Sarcastic comment about the paper's tone and future reception, not adding factual content.

comments.author: boesboes
comments.text: Reminds me of https://thenewinquiry.com/book-of-lamentations/ edit: A review of the DSM as if it where a dystopian novel basically, makes some interesting observations/points
comments.analysis: References a relevant critique of DSM, adding context though without direct analysis of the paper.

comments.author: skeledrew
comments.text: "You look happy. What's wrong?" Ultimate conversation starter.
comments.analysis: A humorous quip that lightly critiques social reactions to happiness but lacks depth.
